entries.name: Antarctica
entries.desc: Earth's southernmost continent, governed by international treaty and accessible only by expedition cruise from Ushuaia or Punta Arenas. No permanent civilian population, no hotels — visitors observe penguin colonies, calving glaciers, and the Weddell Sea pack ice from ships or zodiacs.

photo.alt: Glacier along the Antarctic Peninsula coastline
photo.credit: <!-- photo: antarctica-glacier.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Glacier in Antarctica, Antarctic Peninsula" by PaoMic · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Glacier_in_Antarctica,_Antarctic_Peninsula.JPG -->

entries.name: Patagonia — Los Glaciares
entries.desc: A national park in Argentine Patagonia anchored by the Perito Moreno Glacier — one of the world's few advancing glaciers — and the granite towers above El Chaltén, the trekking capital of Argentina. Reached by flights to El Calafate followed by long road transfers.

photo.alt: Perito Moreno Glacier face in Los Glaciares National Park
photo.credit: <!-- photo: perito-moreno-glacier.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.5 · "Perito Moreno Glacier Patagonia Argentina Luca Galuzzi 2005" by Luca Galuzzi · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Perito_Moreno_Glacier_Patagonia_Argentina_Luca_Galuzzi_2005.JPG -->

entries.name: Bhutan
entries.desc: A landlocked Himalayan kingdom with a controlled-tourism policy requiring visitors to book through licensed operators and pay a daily Sustainable Development Fee. Known for Tiger's Nest Monastery (Paro Taktsang) perched on a 900-meter cliff, pristine dzong fortresses, and one of the world's lowest-traffic trekking networks.

photo.alt: Tiger's Nest Monastery (Paro Taktsang) on its cliffside in the Paro Valley
photo.credit: <!-- photo: tigers-nest-monastery.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Taktsang Palphug Monastery aka Paro Taktsang aka Tiger's Nest, July 2016 13" by Afifa Afrin · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Taktsang_Palphug_Monastery_aka_Paro_Taktsang_aka_Tiger%27s_Nest,_July_2016_13.jpg -->

entries.name: Salar de Uyuni
entries.desc: The world's largest salt flat, sitting at 3,650 meters on the Bolivian altiplano, stretching over 10,000 square kilometers. In the rainy season a thin layer of water creates a mirror effect that reflects the sky. Reached by bus or 4WD from Uyuni town after flights to La Paz or Sucre.

photo.alt: Mirror-like reflection on the Salar de Uyuni salt flat
photo.credit: <!-- photo: salar-de-uyuni.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Reflection on the Salar de Uyuni, bolivia" by Christopher Crouzet · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Reflection_on_the_Salar_de_Uyuni,_bolivia.jpg -->

entries.name: Amazon Rainforest Interior
entries.desc: The deep interior of the world's largest tropical rainforest, accessible only by small river boat from Manaus after a multi-day upstream journey. Rare wildlife including pink river dolphins, jaguar, and hundreds of bird species inhabit a flooded forest landscape where there are no roads.

photo.alt: Aerial view of the Amazon rainforest canopy over the Solimões River
photo.credit: <!-- photo: amazon-rainforest-aerial.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Aerial view of the Amazon Rainforest" by lubasi · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Aerial_view_of_the_Amazon_Rainforest.jpg -->

entries.name: Atacama Desert
entries.desc: The driest non-polar desert on Earth, in northern Chile at 2,400 meters elevation, centered on the town of San Pedro de Atacama. Landscapes include the Valle de la Luna, El Tatio geysers, flamingo-filled salt lakes, and one of the world's best stargazing skies. Reached by flight to Calama followed by road transfer.

photo.alt: Valle de la Luna rock formations near San Pedro de Atacama
photo.credit: <!-- photo: valle-de-la-luna-atacama.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Valle de la Luna, San Pedro de Atacama, Chile." by Freddy Alexander Bugueño Tolmo · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Valle_de_la_Luna,_San_Pedro_de_Atacama,_Chile..jpg -->

entries.name: Patagonia — Torres del Paine
entries.desc: A national park in Chilean Patagonia built around three granite towers rising over 2,800 meters, with the W Trek and O Circuit among the world's most celebrated multi-day hikes. Reached by flight to Punta Arenas then a four-hour bus to Puerto Natales, followed by another transfer into the park.

photo.alt: The three granite towers of Torres del Paine at sunrise
photo.credit: <!-- photo: torres-del-paine.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Torres del paine at sunrise" by Winky · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Torres_del_paine_at_sunrise.jpg -->

entries.name: Tibet
entries.desc: An autonomous region on the Tibetan Plateau averaging over 4,500 meters above sea level, home to the Potala Palace in Lhasa, Namtso Lake, and the base camp route to Mount Everest. Foreign visitors require a Tibet Travel Permit in addition to a Chinese visa, and independent travel is not permitted — all visits must be through an authorized tour operator.

photo.alt: Potala Palace on the Red Hill above Lhasa
photo.credit: <!-- photo: potala-palace.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Tibet-5483 - Potala Palace" by Dennis G. Jarvis · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Tibet-5483_-_Potala_Palace.jpg -->

entries.name: Greenland
entries.desc: The world's largest island, an autonomous Danish territory covered 80 percent by an ice sheet up to 3 kilometers thick. The small coastal settlements — including Ilulissat near a UNESCO-listed icefjord — are connected only by air or boat. No roads link towns. The northern lights, dog-sledding, and glacier hiking are the main draws.

photo.alt: Ilulissat Icefjord, Greenland
photo.credit: <!-- photo: ilulissat-icefjord-greenland.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Ilulissat-Eisfjord 1.jpg" by Jens Bludau · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ilulissat-Eisfjord_1.jpg -->

entries.name: Falkland Islands
entries.desc: A British Overseas Territory in the South Atlantic roughly 500 kilometers east of Patagonia, with a small permanent population in Stanley. Home to large penguin colonies, elephant seals, black-browed albatrosses, and a rugged moorland interior. Reached by a twice-weekly flight from Brize Norton (UK) or a weekly charter from Punta Arenas, or as a stop on Antarctic expedition cruises.

photo.alt: Rockhopper penguins, Falkland Islands
photo.credit: <!-- photo: falkland-islands-rockhopper-penguins.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Falkland Islands Penguins 87.jpg" by Ben Tubby · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Falkland_Islands_Penguins_87.jpg -->

entries.name: Mongolian Steppe & Gobi Desert
entries.desc: A vast landlocked country between Russia and China where nomadic herder culture survives largely unchanged, centered on ger camps across open grassland and the Gobi's sand dunes and saxaul forests. The Flaming Cliffs yielded the first confirmed dinosaur eggs. Ulaanbaatar is one of the world's most isolated capitals, and most interior destinations require long overland drives on unpaved tracks.

photo.alt: Gobi Desert sand dunes, Mongolia
photo.credit: <!-- photo: gobi-desert-dunes-mongolia.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Gobi Desert dunes.jpg" by Richard Mortel · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Gobi_Desert_dunes.jpg -->

entries.name: Namib Desert
entries.desc: Considered the world's oldest desert at around 55 million years old, running 2,000 kilometers along Africa's Atlantic coast. The Sossusvlei dunes — some reaching 300 meters — and the eerie dead trees of Deadvlei are the visual centerpiece. Reached by fly-in from Windhoek or long self-drive on unsealed roads through one of the world's least densely populated countries.

photo.alt: Sossusvlei dunes, Namib Desert
photo.credit: <!-- photo: sossusvlei-dunes-namib-desert.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.5 · "Sossusvlei Dune Namib Desert Namibia Luca Galuzzi 2004.JPG" by Luca Galuzzi · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Sossusvlei_Dune_Namib_Desert_Namibia_Luca_Galuzzi_2004.JPG -->

entries.name: Everest Base Camp
entries.desc: A 12-to-14-day round trek from Lukla (accessed by a famously short mountain airstrip) to the 5,364-meter South Base Camp at the foot of Mount Everest, passing through Sherpa villages, Buddhist monasteries, and the high-altitude hub of Namche Bazaar. The trek requires a Sagarmatha National Park permit and crosses two high passes at altitude.

photo.alt: Everest Base Camp, Nepal
photo.credit: <!-- photo: everest-base-camp-nepal.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 4.0 · "Everest Base Camp on a Stormy Day.jpg" by Daniel Oberhaus · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Everest_Base_Camp_on_a_Stormy_Day.jpg -->

entries.name: Svalbard
entries.desc: A Norwegian Arctic archipelago at 78° North, between the Norwegian mainland and the North Pole. Longyearbyen is the world's northernmost settlement with a commercial airport. Polar bears outnumber people; leaving town without a rifle guide is prohibited. The midnight sun runs from April to August, polar night from October to February. Attractions include glacier hikes, snowmobile expeditions, and Northern Lights from November.

photo.alt: Longyearbyen, Svalbard
photo.credit: <!-- photo: longyearbyen-svalbard.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 3.0 · "Longyearbyen, Svalbard e Jan Mayen - panoramio.jpg" by patano · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Longyearbyen,_Svalbard_e_Jan_Mayen_-_panoramio.jpg -->

entries.name: Papua New Guinea Highlands
entries.desc: A mountainous interior home to hundreds of distinct tribal groups, many maintaining traditional dress, face paint, and cultural practices little changed for centuries. The annual Goroka and Hagen cultural shows bring dozens of tribes together. Infrastructure is limited — most highland communities are accessible only by small plane or multi-day foot trek through dense jungle and steep ridgelines.

photo.alt: Goroka Show tribal dancers, Papua New Guinea
photo.credit: <!-- photo: goroka-show-papua-new-guinea.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Goroka Show - Lastra.jpg" by Anselmo Lastra · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Goroka_Show_-_Lastra.jpg -->

entries.name: Kamchatka Peninsula
entries.desc: A remote peninsula in Russia's Far East projecting into the Pacific, with over 300 volcanoes including 29 active ones, extensive geothermal fields, and some of the world's densest brown bear populations. Accessed by flight to Petropavlovsk-Kamchatsky (about 8 hours from Moscow), then helicopter or boat to the wilderness areas. Foreign visitor numbers remain extremely low.

photo.alt: Koryaksky volcano, Kamchatka Peninsula
photo.credit: <!-- photo: koryaksky-volcano-kamchatka.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY 2.0 · "Kamchatka Volcano Koryaksky (24231533812).jpg" by kuhnmi · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Kamchatka_Volcano_Koryaksky_(24231533812).jpg -->

entries.name: South Georgia
entries.desc: A sub-Antarctic British Overseas Territory in the South Atlantic, accessible only by expedition cruise — there are no flights and no permanent civilian residents. The island hosts the largest king penguin colony on Earth, vast elephant seal harems, and the grave of Ernest Shackleton at Grytviken, where his 1914 Imperial Trans-Antarctic Expedition ended.

photo.alt: Thousands of king penguins covering the beach at Salisbury Plain, South Georgia
photo.credit: <!-- photo: king-penguin-colony-south-georgia.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "King Penguins at Salisbury Plain" by Liam Quinn · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:King_Penguins_at_Salisbury_Plain_(5719368307).jpg -->

entries.name: Pitcairn Islands
entries.desc: The world's most remote inhabited territory — a British Overseas Territory of four islands in the South Pacific, with a permanent population of around 40 people descended from Bounty mutineers and their Tahitian companions. There is no airport; the only way to arrive is by a cargo/passenger supply ship from Mangareva, French Polynesia, a journey of roughly 32 hours each way with departures once or twice a month.

photo.alt: Pitcairn Island's cliffs rising from the South Pacific, seen from the sea
photo.credit: <!-- photo: pitcairn-island-aerial.jpg — Wikimedia Commons Public Domain · "Pitcairn Island NOAA" · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Pitcairn_Island_NOAA.jpg -->

entries.name: Socotra Island
entries.desc: A UNESCO World Heritage island in the Arabian Sea so isolated that a third of its plant life exists nowhere else on Earth, including the unmistakable Dragon Blood Tree with its umbrella-shaped canopy. Often called the Galápagos of the Indian Ocean. Direct flights from Abu Dhabi and Dubai currently operate, though access depends on the ongoing security situation in Yemen.

photo.alt: Dragon Blood Trees with their distinctive umbrella-shaped canopies on Socotra Island
photo.credit: <!-- photo: socotra-dragon-blood-tree.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Dragon's Blood Tree, Socotra Island" by Rod Waddington · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Dragon's_Blood_Tree,_Socotra_Island_(30133804756).jpg -->

entries.name: Victoria Falls Backcountry
entries.desc: While Victoria Falls itself is accessible, the vast surrounding wilderness — Mana Pools National Park on the Zimbabwe side and the Lower Zambezi on the Zambian side — is reached only by small charter plane or long bush road. Canoe safaris on the Zambezi pass within meters of hippos and crocodiles; Mana Pools is one of the few African parks where walking safaris alongside elephant and lion are permitted without a vehicle.

photo.alt: Sunset over the Lower Zambezi River on the Zambia side of the Victoria Falls backcountry
photo.credit: <!-- photo: lower-zambezi-river.jpg — Wikimedia Commons CC BY-SA 2.0 · "Lower Zambezi, Zambia" by Joachim Huber · https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Lower_Zambezi,_Zambia_(2518068461).jpg -->

meta: Charter Plane or Bush Road Only
